title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Rachel Stone, who started a community garden on unused land.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Rachel Stone, who started a community garden on unused land. Rachel, you helped turn an empty piece of land into a garden. How did the idea begin? || Guest: The land was beside my flat, and for years it was just covered in rubbish. People complained about it, but nothing changed. One spring, [20]I saw children playing there and thought it should be a safer, greener place[/20]. I wrote to the council with two neighbours, and we asked if local people could look after it. To my surprise, they said yes, as long as we kept it open to everyone. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: The first job was not planting flowers. It was clearing the ground, and that took weeks. We found broken chairs, old bottles and even part of a bicycle. [21]Getting enough volunteers for the boring cleaning work was the hardest part[/21]. Everyone liked the idea of a garden, but fewer people wanted to spend Saturday mornings filling bags with rubbish. We made it easier by working in short, friendly sessions. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: At first I wanted colourful flowers everywhere, but an experienced gardener gave me good advice. She said we needed plants that would survive if nobody watered them for a few days. [22]We chose herbs because they are useful, smell nice and do not need too much care[/22]. Now people pick mint or rosemary for cooking, and the children love touching the leaves. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: I am proudest of the people who use the garden. [23]Neighbours who had lived in the same street for years finally learnt each other's names[/23]. Before, they passed each other quickly on the stairs. Now they share seeds, tools and cups of tea. The garden is small, but it has made the street feel friendlier and safer in the evenings. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: We did make mistakes. In the first summer, we had too many open meetings, and decisions took forever. [24]Now each small team is responsible for one area, such as watering, tools or children's activities[/24]. They still talk to each other, but they do not need everyone to vote on every small job. That has made the project much easier to manage. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: Next, we want to improve the garden in winter. At the moment, it looks a bit empty after October. [25]We are planning to build a simple shelter so people can meet there when it rains[/25]. It will not be large, but it could be used for seed swaps, children's drawing sessions or just a dry place to sit after work. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like community garden, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Rachel Stone, who started a community garden on unused land. Rachel, you helped turn an empty piece of land into a garden. How did the idea begin?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The land was beside my flat, and for years it was just covered in rubbish. People complained about it, but nothing changed. One spring, I saw children playing there and thought it should be a safer, greener place. I wrote to the council with two neighbours, and we asked if local people could look after it. To my surprise, they said yes, as long as we kept it open to everyone.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The first job was not planting flowers. It was clearing the ground, and that took weeks. We found broken chairs, old bottles and even part of a bicycle. Getting enough volunteers for the boring cleaning work was the hardest part. Everyone liked the idea of a garden, but fewer people wanted to spend Saturday mornings filling bags with rubbish. We made it easier by working in short, friendly sessions.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: At first I wanted colourful flowers everywhere, but an experienced gardener gave me good advice. She said we needed plants that would survive if nobody watered them for a few days. We chose herbs because they are useful, smell nice and do not need too much care. Now people pick mint or rosemary for cooking, and the children love touching the leaves.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I am proudest of the people who use the garden. Neighbours who had lived in the same street for years finally learnt each other's names. Before, they passed each other quickly on the stairs. Now they share seeds, tools and cups of tea. The garden is small, but it has made the street feel friendlier and safer in the evenings.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We did make mistakes. In the first summer, we had too many open meetings, and decisions took forever. Now each small team is responsible for one area, such as watering, tools or children's activities. They still talk to each other, but they do not need everyone to vote on every small job. That has made the project much easier to manage.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Next, we want to improve the garden in winter. At the moment, it looks a bit empty after October. We are planning to build a simple shelter so people can meet there when it rains. It will not be large, but it could be used for seed swaps, children's drawing sessions or just a dry place to sit after work.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like community garden, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: What made Rachel want to improve the land?

options.B: She wanted somewhere to grow vegetables.
options.C: She thought children needed a safer place.

questions.explanation: Rachel says she saw children playing there and thought it should be safer and greener.

questions.question: What was the hardest part at the start?

options.A: finding people to help clean the land

questions.explanation: Rachel says getting enough volunteers for the boring cleaning work was the hardest part.

questions.question: Why did they plant herbs?

options.A: They were the cheapest plants to buy.
options.B: They were useful and easy to look after.
options.C: They made the garden look colourful.

questions.explanation: Rachel says they chose herbs because they are useful and do not need too much care.

questions.question: What is Rachel proudest of?

options.C: helping neighbours get to know each other

questions.explanation: Rachel says neighbours who had lived nearby for years finally learnt each other's names.

questions.question: How is the project organised now?

options.A: Small teams look after different jobs.

questions.explanation: Rachel says each small team is responsible for one area.

questions.question: What do they plan to add to the garden?
questions.explanation: Rachel says they are planning to build a simple shelter so people can meet when it rains.
